source_label: AP Physics 1 — Unit 4 Linear Momentum (workbook + Barron's + 5 Steps + PYQs, vision-extracted)

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: A car of mass m, traveling at speed v, stops in time τ when maximum braking force is applied. Assuming the braking force is independent of mass, what time would be required to stop a car of mass 2m traveling at speed v?

(A) ½τ
(B) τ
(C) √2 τ
(D) 2τ

source_reference.worksheet_name: Barron's AP Physics 1 Premium 2024 (Rideout & Wolf)

ordered_content.value: What braking force is supplied to a 3,000-kg car traveling with a velocity of 35 m/s that is stopped in 12 s?

source_reference.worksheet_name: 5 Steps to a 5 — AP Physics 1 (2024 ed.)

ordered_content.value: A 2-kg coconut falls from the top of a tall tree, 30 m above a person's head. The coconut strikes and comes to rest on the person's head. Justify all answers thoroughly. (a) Calculate the magnitude of the momentum of the coconut just before it hits the person in the head. (b) Calculate the magnitude and direction of the impulse experienced by the coconut in colliding with the person's head. (c) The person's head experienced a force of 10,000 N in the collision. How long was the coconut in contact with the person's head? (Much more than 10 seconds / Just a bit more than one second / Just a bit less than one second / Much less than 1/10 second) (d) In a different situation, explain how it could be possible for an identical coconut dropped from the same height to hit the person's head, but produce less than 10,000 N of force.

question_types.label: Impulse from force-time graph (J = area under F-t)

ordered_content.value: A student obtains data on the magnitude of force applied to an object as a function of time and displays the data on the graph shown. The increase in the momentum of the object between t = 0 s and t = 4 s is most nearly:

ordered_content.value: The graph shows the force on an object of mass M as a function of time. For the time interval 0 to 4 s, the total change in the momentum of the object is:

(A) 40 kg·m/s
(B) 20 kg·m/s
(C) 0 kg·m/s
(D) -20 kg·m/s

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: A 2 kg object initially moving with a constant velocity is subjected to a force of magnitude F in the direction of motion. A graph of F as a function of time t is shown. What is the increase, if any, in the velocity of the object during the time the force is applied?

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 2014 Released Exam

ordered_content.value: (7 points, suggested 13 min) The figure shows two carts with magnets attached, that make up a system. The mass of one cart and magnet is 1 kg and the mass of the other is 5 kg. The carts are initially at rest on a frictionless track. They are released from rest and exert a repulsive force on each other. The track is not quite horizontal, with the right side slightly lower than the left side. The speeds of the carts are measured over a 10 s interval and graphed as momentum vs time for the two carts as they move along the x-axis. (a) Based on the graph, were the measurements started at the instant the carts were released? Justify your answer. (b) Calculate the magnitude of the external force exerted on the system. (c) Suppose the experiment is repeated with different carts, so that the masses of cart plus magnet are 2 kg and 4 kg. Would your answer to part (b) be different with the new masses? Justify your answer.

question_types.label: Kinetic energy and momentum (KE = p²/2m, comparisons)

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: Two pucks P and Q have the same momentum. Q has more mass than P. Which puck can have more kinetic energy if it is:

(A) More mass than P
(B) The same mass as P
(C) More speed than P
(D) The same speed at P

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: Consider two laboratory carts of different masses but identical kinetic energies. Which of the following statements must be correct? Select two answers.

(A) The one with the greatest mass has the greatest momentum
(B) The same impulse was required to accelerate each cart from rest
(C) Both can do the same amount of work as they come to rest
(D) The same amount of force was required to accelerate each cart from rest

options: (A) The one with the greatest mass has the greatest momentum
(B) The same impulse was required to accelerate each cart from rest
(C) Both can do the same amount of work as they come to rest
(D) The same amount of force was required to accelerate each cart from rest

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: A solid metal ball and a hollow plastic ball of the same external radius are released from rest in a large vacuum chamber. When each has fallen 1 meter, they both have the same:

(A) inertia
(B) speed
(C) momentum
(D) change in potential energy

source_reference.worksheet_name: Barron's AP Physics 1 Premium 2024 (Rideout & Wolf)

ordered_content.value: Which of the following expressions, where p represents the linear momentum of the particle, is equivalent to the kinetic energy of a moving particle?

(A) mp²
(B) m²/2p
(C) 2p/m
(D) p²/2m

source_reference.worksheet_name: Barron's AP Physics 1 Premium 2024 (Rideout & Wolf)

ordered_content.value: The product of an object's instantaneous momentum and its acceleration is equal to its

(A) applied force
(B) kinetic energy
(C) power output
(D) net force

source_reference.worksheet_name: Barron's AP Physics 1 Premium 2024 (Rideout & Wolf)

ordered_content.value: (a) Can an object have energy without having momentum? Explain. (b) Can an object have momentum without having energy? Explain.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: A spring is compressed between two objects with unequal masses, m and M, and held together. The objects are initially at rest on a horizontal frictionless surface. When released, which of the following is true?

(A) The total final kinetic energy is zero.
(B) The two objects have equal kinetic energy.
(C) The speed of one object is equal to the speed of the other.
(D) The total final momentum of the two objects is zero.

options: (A) The total final kinetic energy is zero.
(B) The two objects have equal kinetic energy.
(C) The speed of one object is equal to the speed of the other.
(D) The total final momentum of the two objects is zero.
questions.correct_option: (D) The total final momentum of the two objects is zero.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: Two objects having the same mass travel toward each other on a flat surface each with a speed of 1.0 meter per second relative to the surface. The objects collide head-on and are reported to rebound after the collision, each with a speed of 2.0 meters per second relative to the surface. Which of the following assessments of this report is most accurate?

(A) Momentum was not conserved therefore the report is false.
(B) If potential energy was released to the objects during the collision the report could be true.
(C) If the objects had different masses the report could be true.
(D) If the surface was inclined the report could be true.

options: (A) Momentum was not conserved therefore the report is false.
(B) If potential energy was released to the objects during the collision the report could be true.
(C) If the objects had different masses the report could be true.
(D) If the surface was inclined the report could be true.
questions.correct_option: (B) If potential energy was released to the objects during the collision the report could be true.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: A block of mass M is initially at rest on a frictionless floor. The block, attached to a massless spring with spring constant k, is initially at its equilibrium position. An arrow with mass m and velocity v is shot into the block. The arrow sticks in the block. What is the maximum compression of the spring?

(A) 1 km/h
(B) 1.3 km/h
(C) 1.5 km/h
(D) 2.5 km/h

ordered_content.value: A cart with mass 2m has a velocity v before it strikes another cart of mass 3m at rest. The two carts couple and move off with a velocity of:

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: A mass m has speed v. It then collides with a stationary object of mass 2m. If both objects stick together in a perfectly inelastic collision, what is the final speed of the newly formed object?

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: Two bodies of masses 5 and 7 kilograms are initially at rest on a horizontal frictionless surface. A light spring is compressed between the bodies, which are held together by a thin cord. After the spring is released by burning through the thread, the 5 kilogram body has a speed of 0.2 m/s. The speed of the 7 kilogram body (in m/s) is:

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: Which of the following is true when an object of mass m moving on a horizontal frictionless surface hits and sticks to an object of mass M > m, which is initially at rest on the surface?

(A) The collision is elastic.
(B) The momentum of the objects that are stuck together has a smaller magnitude than the initial momentum of the less-massive object.
(C) The speed of the objects that are stuck together will be less than the initial speed of the less-massive object.
(D) The direction of motion of the objects that are stuck together depends on whether the hit is a head-on collision.

options: (B) The momentum of the objects that are stuck together has a smaller magnitude than the initial momentum of the less-massive object.
(C) The speed of the objects that are stuck together will be less than the initial speed of the less-massive object.
(D) The direction of motion of the objects that are stuck together depends on whether the hit is a head-on collision.
questions.correct_option: (C) The speed of the objects that are stuck together will be less than the initial speed of the less-massive object.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: A railroad car of mass m is moving at speed v when it collides with a second railroad car of mass M which is at rest. The two cars lock together instantaneously and move along the track. What is the kinetic energy of the cars immediately after the collision?

(A) ½mv²
(B) ½(M+m)(mv/M)²
(C) (M+m)(Mv/m)²
(D) ½ (M+m)(mv/(m+M))²

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: The two blocks of masses M and 2M shown above initially travel at the same speed v but in opposite directions. They collide and stick together. How much mechanical energy is lost to other forms of energy during the collision?

(A) ½ M v²
(B) 3/4 M v²
(C) 4/3 M v²
(D) 3/2 M v²

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: An object of mass M travels along a horizontal air track at a constant speed v and collides elastically with an object of identical mass that is initially at rest on the track. Which of the following statements is true for the two objects after the impact?

(A) The total momentum is Mv but the total kinetic energy is ½ Mv².
(B) The total momentum is Mv and the total kinetic energy is less than ½ Mv².
(C) The total momentum is less than Mv and the total kinetic energy is ½ Mv².
(D) The momentum of each object is ½ Mv

options: (A) The total momentum is Mv but the total kinetic energy is ½ Mv².
(B) The total momentum is Mv and the total kinetic energy is less than ½ Mv².
(C) The total momentum is less than Mv and the total kinetic energy is ½ Mv².
(D) The momentum of each object is ½ Mv
questions.correct_option: (A) The total momentum is Mv but the total kinetic energy is ½ Mv².

source_reference.worksheet_name: Barron's AP Physics 1 Premium 2024 (Rideout & Wolf)

ordered_content.value: A mass m is attached to a massless spring with a force constant k. The mass rests on a horizontal, frictionless surface. The system is compressed a distance x from the spring's initial position and then released. The momentum of the mass when the spring passes its equilibrium position is given by

(A) √(mkx²)
(B) x√(mk)
(C) √(kx/m)
(D) √(mx/k)

source_reference.worksheet_name: Barron's AP Physics 1 Premium 2024 (Rideout & Wolf)

ordered_content.value: Two blocks with masses 1 kg and 4 kg, respectively, are moving on a horizontal, frictionless surface. The 1-kg block has a velocity of 12 m/s, and the 4-kg block is ahead of it, moving at 4 m/s. The 4-kg block has a massless spring attached to the end facing the 1-kg block. The spring has a force constant k = 1,000 N/m. (a) What is the maximum compression of the spring after the collision? (b) What are the final velocities of the blocks after the collision has taken place?

source_reference.worksheet_name: 5 Steps to a 5 — AP Physics 1 (2024 ed.)

ordered_content.value: Car A has a mass of 1,500 kg and travels to the right with a speed of 20 m/s. Car B initially travels to the left with a speed of 10 m/s. After the vehicles collide, they stick together, moving left with a common speed of 5 m/s. Justify all answers thoroughly. (a) Calculate the mass of Car B. (b) This collision is not elastic. Explain why not. (c) Describe specifically a collision between these two cars with the same initial conditions, but which is not elastic, and in which the cars bounce off one another. (d) Is the collision elastic when Car B remains at rest after the collision?

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 2015 Released Exam

ordered_content.value: (7 points, suggested 13 min) Cart A has mass M and is released from rest at a height 2H on a ramp making angle 2θ. Cart B has mass 2M and is released from rest at a height H on a ramp making angle θ. The carts roll toward each other, have a head-on collision on the horizontal portion of the ramp, and stick together. Wheels are negligible mass; no friction or drag. (a) Indicate whether the carts remain at rest, move to the left, or move to the right after the collision. Explain how you arrived at your answer.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: A bullet of mass m and velocity v_o is fired toward a block of mass 4m. The block is initially at rest on a frictionless horizontal surface. The bullet penetrates the block and emerges with a velocity of v_o/3. (a) Determine the final speed of the block. (b) Determine the loss in kinetic energy of the bullet. (c) Determine the gain in the kinetic energy of the block.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: A block of mass M_1 travels horizontally with a constant speed v_o on a plateau of height H until it comes to a cliff. A toboggan of mass M_2 is positioned on level ground below the cliff as shown above. The center of the toboggan is a distance D from the base of the cliff. (a) Determine D in terms of v_o, H, and g so that the block lands in the center of the toboggan. (b) The block sticks to the toboggan which is free to slide without friction. Determine the resulting velocity of the block and toboggan.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: A block of mass M is resting on a horizontal, frictionless table and is attached as shown above to a relaxed spring of spring constant k. A second block of mass 2M and initial speed v_o collides with and sticks to the first block. Develop expressions for the following quantities in terms of M, k, and v_o. (a) v, the speed of the blocks immediately after impact. (b) x, the maximum distance the spring is compressed.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: (Ballistic pendulum) A 2-kilogram block initially hangs at rest at the end of two 1-meter strings of negligible mass as shown on the left diagram above. A 0.003-kilogram bullet, moving horizontally with a speed of 1000 meters per second, strikes the block and becomes embedded in it. After the collision, the bullet/block combination swings upward, but does not rotate. (a) Calculate the speed v of the bullet/block combination just after the collision. (b) Calculate the ratio of the initial kinetic energy of the bullet to the kinetic energy of the bullet/block combination immediately after the collision. (c) Calculate the maximum vertical height above the initial rest position reached by the bullet/block combination.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: As shown above, a 0.20-kilogram mass is sliding on a horizontal, frictionless air track with a speed of 3.0 meters per second when it instantaneously hits and sticks to a 1.3-kilogram mass initially at rest on the track. The 1.3-kilogram mass is connected to one end of a massless spring, which has a spring constant of 100 newtons per meter. The other end of the spring is fixed. (a) Determine the following for the 0.20-kilogram mass immediately before the impact: (i) Its linear momentum. (ii) Its kinetic energy. (b) Determine the following for the combined masses immediately after the impact: (i) The linear momentum. (ii) The kinetic energy. After the collision, the two masses compress the spring as shown. (c) Determine the maximum compression distance of the spring.

source_reference.worksheet_name: Barron's AP Physics 1 Premium 2024 (Rideout & Wolf)

ordered_content.value: A 2-kg mass moving with a velocity of 7 m/s collides elastically with a 4-kg mass moving in the opposite direction at 4 m/s. The 2-kg mass reverses direction after the collision and has a new velocity of 3 m/s. What is the new velocity of the 4-kg mass?

(A) −1 m/s
(B) 1 m/s
(C) 6 m/s
(D) 4 m/s

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 2016 Released Exam

ordered_content.value: (7 points, suggested 13 min) Two blocks A and B of equal mass are on a frictionless track. Block A, initially moving with speed v_1, has a perfectly elastic collision with block B. Block B has a speed v_1 immediately after the collision, and then it travels around a vertical loop of radius R, where R is much larger than the size of the blocks. The speed of block B at the top of the loop is v_top. (a) Derive an equation for the height h to which block B rises on the ramp after the loop, in terms of m, R, v_top, and g.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: Two identical objects A and B of mass M move on a one-dimensional, horizontal air track. Object B initially moves to the right with speed v_o. Object A initially moves to the right with speed 3v_o, so that it collides with object B. Friction is negligible. Express your answers to the following in terms of M and v_o. (a) Determine the total momentum of the system of the two objects. (b) A student predicts that the collision will be totally inelastic (the objects stick together on collision). Assuming this is true, determine the following for the two objects immediately after the collision: (i) The speed. (ii) The direction of motion (left or right). When the experiment is performed, the student is surprised to observe that the objects separate after the collision and that object B subsequently moves to the right with a speed 2.5v_o. (c) Determine the following for object A immediately after the collision. (i) The speed. (ii) The direction of motion (left or right). (d) Determine the kinetic energy dissipated in the actual experiment.

question_types.label: Inelastic (not perfectly): KE lost in collision

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: As shown in the top view, a disc of mass m is moving horizontally to the right with speed v on a table with negligible friction when it collides with a second disc of mass 2m. The second disc is moving horizontally to the right with speed v/2 at the moment before impact. The two discs stick together upon impact. The kinetic energy of the composite body immediately after the collision is:

source_reference.worksheet_name: Barron's AP Physics 1 Premium 2024 (Rideout & Wolf)

ordered_content.value: A 1-kg object is moving with a velocity of 6 m/s to the right. It collides and sticks to a 2-kg object moving with a velocity of 3 m/s in the same direction. How much kinetic energy was lost in the collision?

source_reference.worksheet_name: Barron's AP Physics 1 Premium 2024 (Rideout & Wolf)

ordered_content.value: During an inelastic collision between two balls, which of the following statements is correct?

(A) Both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved.
(B) Momentum is conserved, but kinetic energy is not conserved.
(C) Momentum is not conserved, but kinetic energy is conserved.
(D) Neither momentum nor kinetic energy is conserved.

options: (A) Both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved.
(B) Momentum is conserved, but kinetic energy is not conserved.
(C) Momentum is not conserved, but kinetic energy is conserved.
(D) Neither momentum nor kinetic energy is conserved.
questions.correct_option: (B) Momentum is conserved, but kinetic energy is not conserved.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: A bullet of mass m is moving horizontally with speed v_o when it hits a block of mass 100m that is at rest on a horizontal frictionless table, as shown above. The surface of the table is a height h above the floor. After the impact, the bullet and the block slide off the table and hit the floor a distance x from the edge of the table. Derive expressions for the following quantities in terms of m, h, v_o, and appropriate constants: (a) the speed of the block as it leaves the table. (b) the change in kinetic energy of the bullet-block system during impact. (c) the distance x. Suppose that the bullet passes through the block instead of remaining in it. (d) State whether the time required for the block to reach the floor from the edge of the table would be greater, less, or the same. Justify your answer. (e) State whether the distance x for the block would be greater, less, or the same. Justify your answer.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: Two carts are held together. Cart 1 is more massive than Cart 2. As they are forced apart by a compressed spring between them, which of the following will have the same magnitude for both carts?

(A) change of velocity
(B) momentum
(C) acceleration
(D) velocity

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: A 50 kg skater at rest on a frictionless rink throws a 2 kg ball, giving the ball a velocity of 10 m/s. Which statement describes the skater's subsequent motion?

(A) 0.4 m/s in the same direction as the ball's motion.
(B) 0.4 m/s in the opposite direction of the ball's motion.
(C) 2 m/s in the same direction as the ball's motion.
(D) 2 m/s in the opposite direction of the ball's motion.

options: (A) 0.4 m/s in the same direction as the ball's motion.
(B) 0.4 m/s in the opposite direction of the ball's motion.
(C) 2 m/s in the same direction as the ball's motion.
(D) 2 m/s in the opposite direction of the ball's motion.
questions.correct_option: (B) 0.4 m/s in the opposite direction of the ball's motion.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: A student initially at rest on a frictionless frozen pond throws a 1 kg hammer in one direction. After the throw, the hammer moves off in one direction and the student moves off in the other. Which of the following correctly describes the above situation?

(A) The hammer will have the momentum of the greater magnitude
(B) The student will have the momentum of the greater magnitude
(C) The hammer will have the greater kinetic energy
(D) The student will have the greater kinetic energy

options: (A) The hammer will have the momentum of the greater magnitude
(B) The student will have the momentum of the greater magnitude
(C) The hammer will have the greater kinetic energy
(D) The student will have the greater kinetic energy
questions.correct_option: (C) The hammer will have the greater kinetic energy

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: Two toy cars with different masses originally at rest are pushed apart by a spring between them. Which TWO of the following statements would be true?

(A) Both toy cars will acquire equal but opposite momenta
(B) Both toy cars will acquire equal kinetic energies
(C) The more massive toy car will acquire the least kinetic energy
(D) The smaller toy car will experience an acceleration of the greatest magnitude

options: (A) Both toy cars will acquire equal but opposite momenta
(B) Both toy cars will acquire equal kinetic energies
(C) The more massive toy car will acquire the least kinetic energy
(D) The smaller toy car will experience an acceleration of the greatest magnitude

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: Two pucks are firmly attached to a stretched spring and are initially held at rest on a frictionless surface, as shown above. The pucks are then released simultaneously. If puck I has three times the mass of puck II, which of the following quantities is the same for the two pucks as the spring pulls the two pucks toward each other?

(A) Speed
(B) Magnitude of acceleration
(C) Kinetic energy
(D) Magnitude of momentum

What are the magnitude and direction of the velocity of the piece having mass 3m?

(A) V/√2, along bisector
(B) V/√2, opposite bisector
(C) (√2 V)/3, along bisector
(D) (√2 V)/3, opposite bisector

As they move apart, the velocity of object A is 5 m/s and the velocity of object B is −2 m/s. The ratio of the mass of object A to the mass of object B, m_A/m_B is:

ordered_content.value: Two carts having masses 1.5 kg and 0.7 kg, respectively, are initially at rest and are held together by a compressed massless spring. When released, the 1.5-kg cart moves to the left with a velocity of 7 m/s. What is the velocity and direction of the 0.7-kg cart?

(A) 15 m/s right
(B) 15 m/s left
(C) 7 m/s left
(D) 7 m/s right

source_reference.worksheet_name: Barron's AP Physics 1 Premium 2024 (Rideout & Wolf)

ordered_content.value: A 50-kg girl stands on a platform with wheels on a frictionless, horizontal surface. The platform has a total mass of 1,000 kg and is attached to a massless spring with a force constant k = 1,000 N/m. The girl throws a 1-kg ball with an initial velocity of 35 m/s at an angle of 30° to the horizontal. (a) What is the recoil velocity of the platform-girl system? (b) What is the elongation of the spring?

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: A massless spring is between a 1-kilogram mass and a 3-kilogram mass as shown above, but is not attached to either mass. Both masses are on a horizontal frictionless table. In one experiment, the 1-kilogram mass is held in place and the spring is compressed by pushing on the 3-kilogram mass. The 3-kilogram mass is then released and moves off with a speed of 10 meters per second. (a) Determine the minimum work needed to compress the spring in this experiment. In a different experiment, the spring is compressed again exactly as above, but this time both masses are released simultaneously and each mass moves off separately at unknown speeds. (b) Determine the final velocity of each mass relative to the cable after the masses are released.

question_types.label: System selection: external forces and momentum conservation

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: An open cart on a level surface is rolling without frictional loss through a vertical downpour of rain, as shown above. As the cart rolls, an appreciable amount of rainwater accumulates in the cart. The speed of the cart will:

(A) increase because of conservation of mechanical energy
(B) decrease because of conservation of momentum
(C) decrease because of conservation of mechanical energy
(D) remain the same because the raindrops are falling perpendicular to the direction of the cart's motion

options: (A) increase because of conservation of mechanical energy
(B) decrease because of conservation of momentum
(C) decrease because of conservation of mechanical energy
(D) remain the same because the raindrops are falling perpendicular to the direction of the cart's motion
questions.correct_option: (B) decrease because of conservation of momentum

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: An empty sled of mass M moves without friction across a frozen pond at speed v₀. Two objects are dropped vertically into the sled one at a time: first an object of mass m and then an object of mass 2m. Afterward the sled moves with speed v₂. What would be the final speed of the sled if the objects were dropped into it in reverse order?

(A) v₂/3
(B) v₂/2
(C) v₂
(D) 2v₂

source_reference.worksheet_name: Barron's AP Physics 1 Premium 2024 (Rideout & Wolf)

ordered_content.value: A cart of mass M is moving with a constant velocity v to the right. A mass m is dropped vertically onto it, and it is observed that the new velocity is less than the original velocity. Explain what has happened in terms of energy, forces, and conservation of momentum (as viewed from different frames of reference).

source_reference.worksheet_name: 5 Steps to a 5 — AP Physics 1 (2024 ed.)

ordered_content.value: A car on a freeway collides with a mosquito, which was initially at rest. Justify all answers thoroughly. (a) Did the total momentum of the car-mosquito system increase, decrease, or remain the same after the collision? (b) Did the momentum of the mosquito increase, decrease, or remain the same after the collision? (c) Did the momentum of the car increase, decrease, or remain the same after the collision? (d) Which changed its speed by more in the collision, the car or the mosquito? (e) Which changed its momentum by more in the collision, the car or the mosquito? (f) Which experienced a greater impulse in the collision, the car or the mosquito? (g) Which experienced a greater magnitude of net force during the collision, the car or the mosquito?

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: Two particles of equal mass m₀, moving with equal speeds v₀ along paths inclined at 60° to the x-axis as shown, collide and stick together. Their velocity after the collision has magnitude:

(A) v₀/4
(B) v₀/2
(C) (√3 v₀)/2
(D) v₀

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: Two balls are on a frictionless horizontal tabletop. Ball X initially moves at 10 meters per second, as shown in Figure I above. It then collides elastically with identical ball Y, which is initially at rest. After the collision, ball X moves at 6 meters per second along a path at 53° to its original direction, as shown in Figure II above. Which of the following diagrams best represents the motion of ball Y after the collision?

(A) 8 m/s along path
(B) 8 m/s along another path
(C) Ball Y diagram
(D) Ball Y diagram

source_reference.worksheet_name: Barron's AP Physics 1 Premium 2024 (Rideout & Wolf)

ordered_content.value: A 0.4-kg disk is initially at rest on a frictionless, horizontal surface. It is hit by a 0.1-kg disk moving horizontally with a velocity of 4 m/s. After the collision, the 0.1-kg disk has a velocity of 2 m/s at an angle of 43° to the positive x-axis. (a) Determine the velocity and direction of the 0.4-kg disk after the collision. (b) Determine the amount of kinetic energy lost in the collision.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: Two objects of masses M_1 = 1 kilogram and M_2 = 4 kilograms are free to slide on a horizontal frictionless surface. The objects collide and the magnitudes and directions of the velocities of the two objects before and after the collision are shown on the diagram above. (sin 37° = 0.6, cos 37° = 0.8, tan 37° = 0.75). (a) Calculate the x and y components (p_x and p_y, respectively) of the momenta of the two objects before and after the collision. (b) Show, using the data that you listed in the table, that linear momentum is conserved in this collision. (c) Calculate the kinetic energy of the two-object system before and after the collision. (d) Is kinetic energy conserved in the collision?

question_types.label: Ball bouncing off surface (impulse with direction reversal)

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: A ball with a mass of 0.50 kg and a speed of 6 m/s collides perpendicularly with a wall and bounces off with a speed of 4 m/s in the opposite direction. The magnitude of the change in momentum of the ball is:

(A) 1 N·s
(B) 5 N·s
(C) 2 N·s
(D) 10 N·s

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: A tennis ball of mass m rebounds from a racquet with the same speed v as it had initially as shown. The magnitude of the momentum change of the ball is:

(A) 0
(B) 2mv
(C) 2mv sinθ
(D) 2mv cosθ

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: A 2 kg ball collides with the floor at an angle θ and rebounds at the same angle and speed as shown above. Which of the following vectors represents the impulse exerted on the ball by the floor?

(A) horizontal arrow
(B) horizontal arrow opposite
(C) angled arrow
(D) vertical arrow

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: A ball of mass m with speed v strikes a wall at an angle θ with the normal, as shown. It then rebounds with the same speed and at the same angle. The impulse delivered by the ball to the wall is:

(A) mv sinθ
(B) mv cosθ
(C) 2mv sinθ
(D) 2mv cosθ

source_reference.worksheet_name: Barron's AP Physics 1 Premium 2024 (Rideout & Wolf)

ordered_content.value: A ball with a mass of 0.15 kg has a velocity of 5 m/s. It strikes a wall perpendicularly and bounces off straight back with a velocity of 3 m/s. The ball underwent a change in momentum equal to

(A) 0.30 kg·m/s
(B) 1.20 kg·m/s
(C) 0.15 kg·m/s
(D) 5 kg·m/s

source_reference.worksheet_name: Barron's AP Physics 1 Premium 2024 (Rideout & Wolf)

ordered_content.value: A 0.1-kg baseball is thrown with a velocity of 35 m/s. The batter hits it straight back with a velocity of 60 m/s. What is the magnitude of the average impulse exerted on the ball by the bat?

question_types.label: Stopping time / contact-time (airbag, bumper, soft landing)

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: How does an air mattress protect a stunt person landing on the ground after a stunt?

(A) It reduces the kinetic energy loss of the stunt person.
(B) It reduces the momentum change of the stunt person.
(C) It shortens the stopping time of the stunt person and reduces the force applied during the landing.
(D) It lengthens the stopping time of the stunt person and reduces the force applied during the landing.

options: (A) It reduces the kinetic energy loss of the stunt person.
(B) It reduces the momentum change of the stunt person.
(C) It shortens the stopping time of the stunt person and reduces the force applied during the landing.
(D) It lengthens the stopping time of the stunt person and reduces the force applied during the landing.
questions.correct_option: (D) It lengthens the stopping time of the stunt person and reduces the force applied during the landing.

source_reference.worksheet_name: Barron's AP Physics 1 Premium 2024 (Rideout & Wolf)

ordered_content.value: Explain why there is more danger when you fall and bounce as opposed to falling without bouncing.

question_types.label: Center of mass motion (invariant under internal forces)

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Physics 1 Practice Workbook — Book 1 (Mechanics, Waves, Electrostatics) — 2014 compilation

ordered_content.value: A boy of mass m and a girl of mass 2m are initially at rest at the center of a frozen pond. They push each other so that she slides to the left at speed v across the frictionless ice surface and he slides to the right. What is the total work done by the children?

(A) mv
(B) ½ mv²
(C) 2mv²
(D) 3mv²
